chunk_summary: A very low long-chain fat diet protocol for pediatric chylothorax minimizes long-chain triglycerides, uses MCT for energy, and includes essential fatty acid supplementation to prevent deficiency. Monitoring and staged reintroduction are critical for effective management.
learning_objectives: Understand the role of a very low long-chain fat diet in managing pediatric chylothorax.
Learn how to prescribe MCT-based formulas and essential fatty acid supplementation.
Identify clinical signs and biochemical markers of essential fatty acid deficiency.
Develop strategies for staged reintroduction of fats post-chylothorax resolution.
key_facts: Long-chain fat intake is restricted to ≤10 g/day in older children, with age-appropriate targets for infants.
MCTs are absorbed directly into the portal system, reducing lymphatic flow.
Essential fatty acids should be supplemented: linoleic acid (2%–4% of total energy) and alpha-linolenic acid (0.25%–0.5% of total energy).
Monitor for signs of deficiency: dermatitis, poor wound healing, hair changes, and growth faltering.
Fat-soluble vitamins may require supplementation during prolonged fat restriction.
Reintroduction of fats should be staged after clinical and radiological resolution of chylothorax.
reference_quote: A very low long-chain fat diet is used to reduce chyle flow in pediatric chylothorax while maintaining adequate energy and protein for healing. (Pediatric Chylothorax Management Algorithm, UNC, 2024)
document_key_topics: Very low long-chain fat diet for pediatric chylothorax
